text: This is Dr. Matthew St. Laurent and today I will be presenting a laparoscopic loop duodenal switch, a modification of the BPD with duodenal switch.

The surgeon inserts a five millimeter optical trocar on the left side to insufflate the abdominal cavity with carbon dioxide, creating a visible working space. Subsequently, additional trocars are introduced in standard positions, employing a five trocar technique similar to that used in sleeve gastrectomy procedures.
The Nathanson liver retractor is introduced to elevate the left lobe of the liver, exposing the upper portion of the stomach. The surgeon identifies the ileocecal valve as the operation begins.

The surgeon follows the ileum upstream for 250 to 300 centimeters, identifying a loop of small bowel intended for anastomosis to the duodenum. This loop is tacked to the upper portion of the greater omentum using a simple suture for easy identification later in the procedure.

The surgeon begins the sleeve gastrectomy by dissecting the lower portion of the stomach using ultrasonic shears. The epiploic vessels along the lateral surface are taken down, gaining access to the retrogastric space. The dissection progresses upstream, dividing short gastric vessels with ultrasonic shears to control bleeding.

The surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to carefully dissect and remove soft adhesions between the posterior aspect of the stomach and the pancreas, facilitating the complete mobilization of the stomach for the sleeve gastrectomy.

The surgeon continues dissection upstream, identifying the spleen on the right side of the screen. The attachments between the stomach and spleen are carefully taken down to avoid bleeding. The dissection proceeds superiorly, exposing the left portion of the diaphragm. The phrenoesophageal attachments are then dissected to fully mobilize the esophagus circumferentially around the diaphragm.
The surgeon dissects in the retroesophageal space, revealing the left portion of the diaphragmatic edge. A moderate-sized hiatal hernia is visible, as previously diagnosed.

The surgeon rotates the stomach and opens the right side of the omental window. The surgical team works towards the right cruce of the diaphragm, taking down the phrenoesophageal attachments to mobilize the esophagus away from the diaphragmatic edge. An instrument on the left holds the right cruce of the diaphragm as the attachments are being dissected.

The surgeon dissects in the posterior space, creating a retrogastric window across to the opposite side. An articulating banding instrument is inserted to elevate the esophagus, providing counter traction for thorough dissection. The posterior vagus nerve is visible and preserved during the dissection. Loose areolar tissue is removed, exposing both columns of the diaphragm. The esophagus and stomach are completely dissected away from the diaphragm, revealing the right cruce, aorta, and left cruce of the diaphragm.

The surgeon performs a posterior repair of the diaphragm by re-approximating the diaphragmatic edges using non-absorbable sutures in a figure-of-eight pattern. Multiple sutures are placed to close the diaphragmatic defect, preventing migration of the gastric pouch into the chest cavity.

The surgeon locates three to four centimeters of the esophagus below the diaphragm, ensuring optimal function of the lower esophageal sphincter. The diaphragmatic defect is carefully closed to avoid postoperative swallowing difficulties and reflux.

After closing the diaphragmatic defect, the surgeon uses ultrasonic shears to dissect the epiploic vessels along the lateral border of the stomach. The dissection proceeds distally, freeing the distal stomach and incorporating four to five centimeters of the duodenum.

The distal stomach is fully mobilized, with dissection extending at least four centimeters beyond the pylorus. An articulating banding instrument is used to mobilize the first portion of the duodenum. The pylorus is visible, and the resection site for sleeve gastrectomy is identified approximately four centimeters beyond the pylorus.

The surgeon begins the resection approximately one to two centimeters proximal to the pylorus, performing the first two firings of the stapler without a calibrating tube to prevent kinking or twisting of the distal stomach.

The surgeon places the third firing of the stapler on the stomach and clamps it down, ensuring not to narrow the incisor at the sharp angulation of the stomach's lower border. A 42 French bougie calibrating tube is then inserted into the distal stomach to assist in forming the appropriate size pouch.

The surgeon places the calibrating tube into the distal stomach and fires the third stapler to continue the resection. Subsequent firings of the endogii complete the resection along the vertical portion of the sleeve, tightly hugging the calibrating tube for postoperative restriction. The resection progresses up to the junction of the esophagus and stomach, ensuring staples are not placed on the esophagus to prevent leaks. Approximately four centimeters of the esophagus are visible below the diaphragm. The tissue reinforcement strip is divided, and the specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity.

After extracting the specimen, the surgeon reintroduces the trocar. The gastric staple line is visually inspected for hemostasis, ensuring no bleeding is present. The bougie calibrating tube is then carefully removed from the stomach.

The surgeon visually identifies the pylorus and proceeds to resect the duodenum just distal to it. Following the division of the duodenum, the surgical team prepares to initiate the duodenal ileal anastomosis.

The surgeon re-identifies the loop of ileum and divides the fixation suture. The loop of small bowel is then positioned adjacent to the duodenum for anastomosis. A hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis is performed, starting with a posterior row of running 2-0 silk suture along the duodenum's width to create a wide opening and prevent strictures.

The surgeon begins at the superior aspect of the duodenum, working downward to incorporate the entire ileal loop. After completing the posterior layer of sutures, innerotomies are created in both the duodenum and ileum distally, followed by the formation of the inner row of sutures.

The surgeon uses an absorbable 3-0 Vicryl suture to form the inner row of the anastomosis, running the suture from top to bottom along the entire length. After completing the posterior inner row, the anterior inner row is formed using the same type of suture, visibly completing the anterior row of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anterior outer row of sutures using a non-absorbable two-oh silk suture in a running fashion, closing the outer layer of the anastomosis from top to bottom. The wide anastomosis is visible, designed to limit the risk of stricture.

The surgeon performs an upper endoscopy to evaluate the staple line and the anastomosis, placing the anastomosis under a saline bath while advancing the endoscope through the efferent and afferent limbs. An air insufflation test is conducted to check for leaks along the staple line, confirming hemostasis. The anastomosis is reinforced with fibrin sealant, and a Jackson Pratt drain is inserted along the lower border of the stomach up to the apex of the spleen.
The surgeon places a Jackson Pratt drain to remove any leftover abdominal irrigation and checks for leaks in the early postoperative period. Following this, the abdominal closure is initiated.

The surgeon removes the larger trocars and closes the fascial defects using a suture needle passer and absorbable ovicral sutures, aiming to reduce the risk of postoperative hernia. The smaller trocars remain in place without closure due to their minimal risk of hernia formation.
The surgeon aspirates the remaining air from the abdominal cavity, marking the completion of the abdominal operation.
